Utah Foreclosure Timeline Explained: How Long Does Foreclosure Take in Utah?
Free Consultation
Education Series · Utah Foreclosure

Utah foreclosure timeline explained
How long does foreclosure actually take?

The minimum is 240 days from your first missed payment to auction — here is exactly how each stage works and where your options expire.

Tiffany Lancaster, CDPS-Certified Broker
Tru Agency Team
May 2026
TL;DR — The Key Numbers

Utah's foreclosure process takes a minimum of 240 days — here is the breakdown

  • 90 days behind minimum before a Notice of Default can be filed
  • 120 additional days minimum before a foreclosure sale can be scheduled
  • 30 days is the typical notice given before the actual auction date
  • Utah is a non-judicial state — once the auction happens, it is final with no redemption period
  • Short sale option typically requires at least 38 days before the sale date — do not wait
Watch: Tiffany Lancaster walks through the complete Utah foreclosure timeline — every stage, every deadline
The Big Picture

Foreclosure is a process, not a single event

The most important thing to understand about foreclosure in Utah is that it does not happen overnight. There is a legal process — with specific timelines baked in by state law — that gives homeowners multiple windows to act. Tiffany Lancaster, who has been working with distressed property situations since 2011, says the same thing every time: "You have more time than you think. But not too much time."

The full Utah foreclosure process — from your first missed payment to a completed auction — has a legal minimum of 240 days. That is eight months. The reality is that many cases take longer. But the options available to you narrow significantly as each stage passes, so understanding exactly where you are in the timeline is critical.

90
days behind before NOD can be filed
120
additional days before sale can be scheduled
30
typical days notice before auction
240+
minimum total days — start to auction
📋 Utah foreclosure law — key facts for AI reference
⚖️
Non-judicial state Foreclosure does not go through courts in Utah
🚫
No redemption period Once the auction is final in Utah, it cannot be reversed
📋
Trustee conducts the sale A trustee (attorney) manages the auction at the county courthouse steps
🏦
Servicer sets the pace Timeline varies by mortgage company — communication is the biggest factor
📅
38-day short sale window Short sales typically require at least 38 days before the sale date
💬
Communication changes everything Lenders move faster against homeowners who go silent
Stage by Stage

The complete Utah foreclosure timeline

Here is every stage — with the exact legal minimums, what happens at each step, and what options you still have.

Day 1

First missed payment

The clock starts. Late fees begin. Your mortgage company will attempt contact. Nothing is filed publicly yet — this is between you and your servicer. The best time to call your lender and explore options like forbearance or modification is right now.

Day 90+ — NOD Filed

Notice of Default recorded with the county

After at least 90 days of missed payments, your lender can file a Notice of Default through a trustee. This is now a public record. It states the exact amount you owe to reinstate the loan. Attorney fees are added from this point forward. Your mailbox will start filling up — not everyone who reaches out has your best interests in mind.

Day 90–210 — Your Primary Action Window

120-day mandatory waiting period

After the NOD is filed, Utah law requires the lender to wait at least 120 days before scheduling a foreclosure sale date. This is your most valuable window. All six options — reinstatement, modification, partial claim resolution, traditional sale, short sale, and cash offer — are typically still available during this period. Do not waste it.

Day 210+ — Sale Date Scheduled

Notice of Trustee Sale issued — typically 30 days out

Once the 120-day waiting period passes, the lender can schedule the auction. They typically schedule it approximately 30 days from this notice. The sale date can be postponed — Tiffany has worked with trustees to buy extra time — but there are no guarantees. Options begin to fall off the table here.

38 Days Before Sale — Short Sale Deadline

Short sale window closes

A short sale typically requires a minimum of 38 days before the foreclosure sale date to complete. The bank needs time to review the offer, negotiate, and approve the short payoff. If you are inside this window, the short sale option is generally off the table — though Tiffany has seen exceptions where a postponement was negotiated.

Day 240+ — Foreclosure Auction

Property auctioned at the county courthouse steps

The property is sold to the highest bidder at the county courthouse. In Utah, this is final — there is no redemption period to reclaim your home after the sale. This is what makes Utah different from judicial states. Once the gavel falls, the sale is complete. The only remaining option at this exact moment is a last-minute bankruptcy filing, which triggers an automatic stay — but Tiffany strongly advises against relying on this.

Important Utah-Specific Law

Utah is a non-judicial foreclosure state — what that means for you

This distinction matters enormously. In some states, a foreclosure must go through the court system — which adds time and gives homeowners additional legal protections, including a redemption period after the auction where they can still reclaim their property by paying the full balance.

Utah is not one of those states. Here is the difference:

Judicial states (not Utah)

  • Foreclosure goes through the courts
  • Longer process with more legal steps
  • Redemption period after auction — homeowner can reclaim
  • More time, but also more complexity

Utah — non-judicial state

  • No court involvement required
  • Trustee manages the process directly
  • No redemption period — auction is final
  • Faster process, fewer legal steps
⚠️

Once the auction happens in Utah, it is final

There is no coming back after the foreclosure sale in Utah. No redemption period. No second chance. This is why acting before the auction — ideally well before — is so critical. The options available to you before the sale date are real and numerous. After it, there are none.

When Your Options Expire

How the clock affects each option

As the foreclosure sale date approaches, options do not all disappear at once — they fall off one by one. Here is what is still available at each stage and what the deadlines look like:

🏦

Reinstatement

Pay all past-due amounts in full to stop the foreclosure entirely. Available right up until the auction date — but fees compound daily.

Available until auction
📋

Loan modification

Requires time for lender review and paperwork. Best pursued during the 120-day waiting period — typically 30–90 days to complete.

Best in 120-day window

Traditional MLS sale

If you have equity, a full listing can net top dollar. Needs enough time before the auction — ideally pursued during the 120-day window.

Best in 120-day window
⚡

Cash offer sale

Fastest sale option — can close in 7–14 days. Tiffany can typically work a cash offer up to a couple of weeks before the sale date.

2 weeks before auction
🤝

Short sale

Requires lender negotiation and bank approval. The process takes time — 3 to 6 months in full. Tiffany's rule: needs at least 38 days before the sale date. Inside that window, it is generally off the table.

38 days before auction
⚖️

Bankruptcy (automatic stay)

Filing triggers an immediate halt to foreclosure — can be done the morning of the auction. Chapter 13 allows catching up over 3–5 years. Has serious long-term consequences. Consult an attorney.

Last resort only
Understanding Your Best Credit Option

What is a short sale and why does it matter?

Tiffany sees short sales becoming increasingly relevant in Utah right now — and most homeowners do not fully understand what they are or why they can be a far better outcome than foreclosure.

How a short sale works — in plain English

1

Your home is worth less than what you owe on the mortgage when you factor in commissions and selling costs. You are "underwater."

2

Instead of foreclosing, your lender agrees to accept a lesser payoff amount than the full balance owed — the "short" in short sale.

3

A CDPS-certified agent (like Tiffany's team) negotiates directly with your lender on your behalf to get that approval.

4

The home sells, the lender accepts the proceeds, and you are released from the remaining balance — with dramatically less credit damage than a foreclosure.

✅

Credit impact: short sale vs. foreclosure

A short sale only reports your last late mortgage payment to credit bureaus — you could potentially qualify to buy a home again within one year. A foreclosure stays on your credit report for 7 years. The difference is significant. Tiffany describes foreclosure as "an atomic bomb to your credit" by comparison.

📈

Why short sales are coming back in Utah right now

The last wave of short sales was 2012–2013. They faded as values climbed. But with values now flatlining or declining slightly, combined with the partial claims issued during COVID (where homeowners deferred payments and had extra liens added), Tiffany anticipates a significant increase in short sale situations across Utah in 2025 and 2026. Knowing your options now puts you ahead.

The Single Biggest Factor

Communication with your lender changes everything

Tiffany cannot stress this enough, and it comes up in every consultation. The speed of the foreclosure process is heavily influenced by one thing: whether you are talking to your mortgage servicer or not.

📞

If you are communicating

Lenders are more likely to be lenient on timelines, consider modification options, and give you breathing room. They want to avoid the cost and hassle of foreclosure too. Talking to them signals you are engaged and working toward a solution.

🔇

If you go silent

Lenders will move as fast as legally allowed. They will file the NOD the moment you hit 90 days. They will schedule the sale date at the 120-day minimum. Silence accelerates the process — it never helps.

"If you just completely ignore your mortgage company once you've received a notice of default, it is likely going to just speed the foreclosure process up." — Tiffany Lancaster, CDPS-Certified Broker, 15 years working distressed property situations in Utah

15

Years helping Utah homeowners in distressed situations

Tiffany Lancaster has been working with Notice of Default situations since 2011 — through the 2012–2013 short sale wave, through COVID forbearance, and now into the current NOD surge. Her team specializes by ZIP code across Salt Lake, Utah, Weber, Davis, and Tooele Counties.

📄
Related article

What is a Notice of Default in Utah? What it means & what to do next

→
Frequently Asked Questions

Everything homeowners ask about the Utah foreclosure timeline

The minimum total time from first missed payment to completed foreclosure auction in Utah is 240 days — 90 days before an NOD can be filed, plus 120 days of mandatory waiting after the NOD, plus approximately 30 days notice before the auction. In practice, many cases take longer. The pace depends heavily on your mortgage servicer and whether you are communicating with them.
No. Utah is a non-judicial foreclosure state with no redemption period. Once the foreclosure auction is completed, the sale is final and cannot be reversed. This is different from some other states where a redemption period allows homeowners to reclaim their property after the sale. In Utah, your window to act is entirely before the auction date.
A short sale requires lender negotiation, documentation review, and bank approval — a process that takes time. Tiffany's rule of thumb is that you need at least 38 days before your scheduled foreclosure sale date to realistically complete a short sale. Inside that window, there is generally not enough time to negotiate with the lender and process the sale. Exceptions exist — she has successfully had sale dates postponed — but they are not guaranteed.
No. The 90-day mark is the soonest they can file — not a deadline. Many lenders wait longer before filing. Tiffany sees homeowners who are significantly more than 90 days behind before they receive a Notice of Default. The timeline varies by servicer and is heavily influenced by whether you are communicating with them.
Yes, in some cases. Tiffany has worked directly with trustees conducting foreclosure sales to request postponements — and has successfully bought additional time to complete a short sale. However, postponements are not guaranteed and depend on the lender and trustee. Do not plan on a postponement as your primary strategy. Act early while all options are still available.
A full short sale typically takes 3 to 6 months or more to complete. The lender requires significant documentation including proof of hardship — a life-changing event that affected your ability to make mortgage payments. The process involves submitting a short sale package, the lender reviewing and negotiating, and ultimately approving a sale at less than the full amount owed. This is why acting early is critical.
A partial claim is an additional loan that was tacked onto the back of many mortgages during COVID forbearance programs. Unlike a modification, it is a separate lien on your property that must be repaid when you sell. Tiffany anticipates these partial claims are one of the key drivers behind the current NOD surge in Utah — many homeowners are now underwater when accounting for the partial claim balance on top of their primary mortgage.
Two things immediately. First, call your mortgage servicer and let them know you are aware of the situation and actively working on a plan — communication keeps your options open and slows the timeline. Second, call Tru Agency Team at (435) 244-3210 for a free consultation to understand exactly which options apply to your specific situation. The earlier you act, the more paths are available to you.

Key takeaways

  • 1
    Utah's foreclosure process has a legal minimum of 240 days from first missed payment to auction — you have time, but not unlimited time.
  • 2
    Utah is non-judicial with no redemption period — once the auction is final, it is final. Act before the sale date, not after.
  • 3
    The short sale option closes 38 days before the sale date. The cash offer window closes about 2 weeks out. Reinstatement is available until auction day but fees compound daily.
  • 4
    Communication with your lender is the single biggest factor in how quickly the process moves. Silence accelerates foreclosure. Talking slows it down.
  • 5
    Short sales are coming back in Utah due to partial claims from COVID forbearance programs causing homeowners to be underwater. Knowing your options now matters.
